perturbation.original_text: Proprietary Information which may be supplied and is protected under this Agreement includes, but is not limited to: all business plans, specifications, reports, manuals, data books, computer programs, techniques, employment arrangements, and all other business practices or information of a private nature on any media whatsoever, and any derivatives of the foregoing, including, without limitation, any notes, analyses, compilations, studies, memoranda or other documents prepared by the Recipient, or its Representatives, which contain, reflect or are based on, in whole or in part, such proprietary information (collectively, "Proprietary Information").
perturbation.changed_text: Proprietary Information which may be supplied and is protected under this Agreement includes only tangible items clearly marked as "Confidential", such as physical documents and electronic storage devices. No other form of information is protected.
perturbation.explanation: This change severely restricts the definition of 'Proprietary Information' to exclude intangible forms of information, business plans, and unlabelled data. This is likely to contradict established trade secret laws.
perturbation.contradicted_law: The Uniform Trade Secrets Act (UTSA), which protects a broad range of confidential business information.
perturbation.law_explanation: By limiting 'Proprietary Information' to only labelled, tangible items, the modified text fails to protect the broader category of trade secrets as defined by the UTSA, which includes intangible business information that provides a competitive edge. This contradicts the purpose of trade secret law and makes it harder to enforce the NDA.

perturbation.justification: The modified contract text severely restricts the definition of 'Proprietary Information' by limiting it to only tangible items clearly marked as "Confidential." This directly contradicts the broader understanding and protection of trade secrets, which typically include intangible business information. The Law Explanation supports this by stating that the revised text fails to protect the wider scope of trade secrets under the UTSA, which include intangible business information that provides a competitive edge. This aligns with the 'Misaligned Terminology - Legal Contradiction' definition, as the changed terminology deviates from legally recognized definitions related to trade secrets, potentially making the contract unenforceable. The change substantially weakens the protection of proprietary information.

perturbation.original_text: Recipient agrees to keep confidential or protect such Proprietary Information as it would its own confidential and proprietary information at the highest level of proprietary material.
perturbation.changed_text: Recipient agrees to keep confidential or protect such Proprietary Information as it deems necessary, at its sole discretion.
perturbation.explanation: This change makes the level of protection for Proprietary Information discretionary, potentially contradicting the reasonable efforts standard often required in confidentiality agreements.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Duty of Care in Confidentiality Agreements.
perturbation.law_citation: E.I. du Pont de Nemours & Co. v. Christopher, 249 U.S.P.Q. 167 (5th Cir. 1986)
perturbation.law_explanation: The original text implies a reasonable standard of care, but the modified text allows the Recipient to protect the information only as it 'deems necessary.' This undermines the implied duty of care that is standard in confidentiality agreements and contradicts legal precedent which requires reasonable measures to protect trade secrets.

perturbation.justification: The change from protecting Proprietary Information at the 'highest level' to protecting it as the Recipient 'deems necessary' introduces a legal contradiction. The original text implies a reasonable standard of care, but the modified text allows the Recipient to protect the information only as it 'deems necessary.' This undermines the implied duty of care that is standard in confidentiality agreements and contradicts legal precedent which requires reasonable measures to protect trade secrets. This falls under the Misaligned Terminology - Legal Contradiction because the term "highest level of proprietary material" has a standard in law, which is now misaligned.

perturbation.original_text: All Proprietary Information, including all applicable intellectual property rights residing in the Proprietary Information (including without limitation, patents, copyrights, industrial designs, trademarks and trade secrets), unless otherwise specified in writing by Disclosing Party, are and shall remain the exclusive property of Disclosing Party, including any and all reproductions in any form of said Proprietary Information.
perturbation.changed_text: All Proprietary Information, excluding trade secrets, unless otherwise specified in writing by Disclosing Party, are and shall remain the exclusive property of Disclosing Party, including any and all reproductions in any form of said Proprietary Information.
perturbation.explanation: Excluding trade secrets from the proprietary information to be exclusively owned by the disclosing party.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Trade secret law, specifically regarding ownership and misappropriation.
perturbation.law_explanation: Trade secrets are still the proprietary information of the company by default, so by excluding them it causes a contradiction with the common law. It weakens the protection offered.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 404 | Berkman Klein Center Skip to the main content Page Cannot Be Found Brave traveler, your journey on our site has brought you to an abrupt dead end. Perhaps you wish to explore one of these other options? About Us Get Involved Events Community Publications Projects & Tools Last updated Mar 27, 2024
perturbation.justification: The original text explicitly includes "trade secrets" within the definition of Proprietary Information that remains the exclusive property of the Disclosing Party. The changed text excludes trade secrets from this definition. The Law Explanation confirms that trade secrets are proprietary information of a company. While the scraped snippets do not explicitly define trade secrets, the Law Explanation indicates that excluding them weakens the protection offered to the disclosing party which is a legal contradiction. Therefore, the modification creates a direct conflict with common law principles protecting trade secrets as proprietary information.

perturbation.original_text: The provisions of this Agreement pertaining to Proprietary Information disclosed under this Agreement shall continue and survive for a period of three (3) years after the termination of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: The provisions of this Agreement pertaining to Proprietary Information disclosed under this Agreement shall continue and survive indefinitely, regardless of whether the information becomes publicly available.
perturbation.explanation: This attempts to extend the non-disclosure obligations indefinitely, even if the Proprietary Information enters the public domain. This contradicts the core principle that information in the public domain is not protectable under trade secret law.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Public Domain Doctrine in Trade Secret Law.
perturbation.law_explanation: Trade secret protection ceases when information becomes publicly available through legitimate means. By attempting to maintain indefinite confidentiality even after public disclosure, the modified text directly contradicts established trade secret law principles and creates an unenforceable provision.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: public domain | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you public domain The public domain includes every creative work that is no longer protected by a copyright , trademark , or patent . Creative works that are no longer protected are owned by the general public rather than the original creator. As such, the work is free to be copied, performed, or otherwise used by anyone. As stated on the Stanford University Libraries site , creative works most commonly become public domain in the four following ways: (1) the copyright expires, (2) failure to properly renew a copyright, (3) the work is placed in the public domain deliberately by the copyright owner, and (4) the work was not of a type that can be protected by copyright. Further, certain transformative uses of the creative work can result in a new copyright owned by the one who created it. That being said, only the aspect of the use that is transformative can be copyrighted, not the public domain work itself. For example, all of Shakespeare’s works are in the public domain. While no one could claim a new copyright on any individual play, a new creator could copyright the specific arrangement of a collection of the plays. Further, new works based on the original works but with a creative new take can be copyrighted, such as the 1999 film “10 Things I Hate About You” based on “The Taming of the Shrew.” [Last reviewed in July of 2020 by the Wex Definitions Team ] Wex PROPERTY wex definitions intellectual property copyright law patent law trademark law Wex Toolbox
perturbation.justification: The modified text extends non-disclosure obligations indefinitely, even if the proprietary information becomes publicly available. This directly contradicts the principle that information in the public domain is not protected by intellectual property law. The provided law snippet explicitly states that the public domain includes creative work no longer protected by copyright, trademark, or patent and is free to be used by anyone, supporting the assessment that attempting to indefinitely protect information, even after it enters the public domain, creates a legal contradiction.
